quotes.text: Jerusalem is the "valley of vision," the source of these burdens. The largest of the valleys in Jerusalem, the Kidron, is sometimes called "the valley of vision" (Isa. 22:1). The prophet was distressed by revelry in the city when there were inadequate preparations for siege (Isa. 22:2-10). Their preparations for saving water from the important spring in a "ditch" or pool would not be as vital for them as trust in the Lord, the "maker" of the spring (Isa. 22:11). Isaiah urged the people to repent of indulgence and neglect. He made an example of a government official, Shebna, who was preparing a sepulchre, warning that he would likely die in exile. Isaiah made a symbol of Shebna's replacement, Eliakim. His name means "God shall cause to arise," anticipating the Savior, who holds the "key of the house of David" but was fastened "as a nail in a sure place" until the burden of the Atonement was complete. Upon Him rests "all the glory of his father's house." Isaiah recommended depending on Him for everlasting security (Isa. 22:20-25). Gethsemane is in the Kidron valley, that "valley of vision.
quotes.speaker: Ellis T. Rasmussen
quotes.source: A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament
quotes.attr: Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 517

quotes.text: Thou that art full of stirs, a tumultuous city, a joyous city. Jerusalem, normally a city full of joy, now mourns the loss of her children.
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.196

quotes.text: Therefore said I, Look away from me; I will weep bitterly. The personal pronoun I now replaces you and your, which are used in the first three verses to refer to Jerusalem. I refers to Isaiah, who weeps bitterly and demonstrates great emotion as he witnesses this vision of destruction (21:3-4).
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.196

quotes.text: For it is a day of trouble, and of treading down. The language here refers to the pending destruction of Jerusalem and parallels the language used when prophets speak of the Second Coming. Joel, for instance, called it "a day of darkness and of gloominess, a day of clouds and of thick darkness" (Joel 2:2).
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.196

notes: uncovered --took off for the battle the leather covering of the shield, intended to protect the embossed figures on it from dust or injury during the march. "The quiver" and "the shield" express two classes--light and heavy armed troops.
Elam =" eternity" a province east of Babylon and northeast of the lower Tigris

quotes.text: Kir uncovered the shield. Kir was a Mesopotamian city situated east of the Tigris River on the main highway that ran from Elam to Babylon. A leather covering protected a warrior's shield when it was not in use, and the phrase uncovered the shield indicates that Kir's warriors held their shields in readiness; it parallels the anticipation of Elam's soldiers who had their arrows removed from the quivers, ready for an impending battle.
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.197

quotes.text: Elam bare the quiver with chariots of men and horsemen. In this passage, Elam's famed archers (Jer. 49:34-39) pull their arrows from their quivers and set them in their bows, ready to battle against Jerusalem. Earlier, Isaiah heard the war cry of an army captain commanding Elam to go to battle against Babylon (21:2).
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.197

notes: the covering - the veil of Judah shall be taken off i.e. exposing to shame as a captive (Isa 47:3; Na 3:5).
breaches of the city of David [The Jews prepared for the war by repairing weaknesses in Jerusalem's walls.]

quotes.text: Ye have seen also the breaches of the city of David. The Jews prepared for the war by repairing weaknesses in Jerusalem's walls.
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.197

quotes.text: Ye made also a ditch. Jerusalem's inhabitants created a channel to move water from outside the city to the inside so water could be obtained during a siege (22:9).
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.197

notes: Their attitude was to seek pleasure and eat and drink; for tomorrow we die (see also 2 Ne. 28:8; 1 Cor. 15:32), which is the attitude held by those who do not believe in God or in a postmortal existence.

quotes.text: Surely this iniquity shall not be purged from you till ye die. The New International Version reads, "'Til your dying day this sin will not be atoned for." In very strong language Isaiah testifies that Judah has gone beyond the point of repentance and will not receive forgiveness in this life for her sins. His revelation closes with the affirmation that it is the Lord who has spoken, "saith the Lord God of hosts.
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.198

notes: This prophecy rests on the heads of two historical characters, Shebna and Eliakim, who were Isaiah's contemporaries and likely acquainted with him. The section consists of two parts. First, Isa. 22:15-19 sets forth certain details pertaining to Shebna, an important and influential government official or treasurer (22:15; 37:2) who served in the court of Hezekiah, king of Judah. Shebna, a proud man of wealth, is rebuked by the Lord through Isaiah for his haughtiness and attempt to build for himself a monumental tomb. Therefore, Isaiah prophesies that a judgment will fall upon his head (22:17-19): Shebna will lose his important position in the kingdom of Judah; he will be violently captured and carried into captivity; his glory will become shame, and he will die in exile. Shebna symbolizes all wicked people of his day and ours. He is an example of what many individuals have become: "He was, therefore, individually what the nation was collectively: wedded to present satisfactions and self-confident in the face of the future." Second, Isa. 22:20-25 pertains to the righteousness and blessing of Eliakim, who was a priest and official in Hezekiah's house (36:3; 37:2). This section is of great import to the reader because Eliakim's life and ministry parallels Jesus Christ's. Eliakim serves as a type of Jesus in several ways: as Eliakim
Judgment Upon Shebna and Blessings Upon Eliakim (22:15-25)

quotes.text: He will surely violently turn and toss thee like a ball into a large country. This is a continuation of the prophecy concerning eventual exile that is formulated in Isa. 22:17: Shebna/Judah, like a ball, will be violently kicked and bounced about on their way to exile.
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.200

quotes.text: I will call my servant Eliakim. Eliakim, the priest, receives a call and replaces Shebna as the servant. Now he typifies Jesus Christ, who is also called "servant" (42:1; Matt. 12:18) and who serves Heavenly Father (Ps. 40:8; John 8:29).
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.201

notes: I will commit thy government into his hand. This pertains to Eliakim, but it is also a messianic statement about Jesus, who received "the throne of David," a "kingdom," and "the government . . . upon his shoulder" (9:6-7). Hand represents power or authority.

quotes.text: I will clothe him with thy robe/girdle. Eliakim (or Christ) receives the garments of priesthood authority, kingship, and righteousness. The robe and girdle refer to the temple's sacred vestments (see commentary on 9:6).
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.201

notes: The power of the keys consisted not only in the supervision of the royal chambers, but also in the decision of who was and who was not to be received into the king's service. There is a resemblance here therefore of the giving of the keys of the kingdom to Peter, under the New Testament. The binding and the loosing, is the opening and the shutting.

the key of the house of David will I lay upon his shoulder. The key, which represents authority and power (Matt. 16:19; Rev. 3:7), opens the door to the heavenly temple. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and

fasten (Heb. taqa‘) to blow, clap, strike, sound, thrust, give a blow, blast. In Isaiah 22:22, 25 the word for fasten is taqa'. It can be translated as 'knock' or clang or make a knocking sound. It also means a primitive root to drive (a nail); by implication , to become bondsman by handclasping

quotes.text: I will fasten him as a nail in a sure place. This image pertains to Christ's crucifixion. "'The nail fastened in a sure place,' remains a mystery to the world, and will, but the wise understand." (Times and Seasons 5 (1844): 748.)
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.202

notes: removed, and be cut down, and fall; and the burden that [was] upon it shall be cut off: for the LORD hath spoken [it].
The Song of the Destruction of Tyre and Sidon (23:1-14)

quotes.text: Isa. 23 is the last of nine prophetic warnings against the heathen nations surrounding Israel. Isaiah began this series of pronouncements in chapter 13 with a prophecy against Babylon; he concludes it in this chapter with a prophecy against Tyre. These two cities symbolize worldliness-Tyre was the center of commerce and a leading sea power; Babylon was the center of politics and a leading land power. Both were condemned for pride (13:11; 23:9) and referred to as harlots (23:15-17; D&C 86:3). The prophecy against Babylon attacks worldliness; the prophecy against Tyre strikes at materialism. Both are types of the world in our day. Isaiah prophesies that the merchants of Tyre will learn of the destruction of their homeland and mourn (23:1). He uses colorful descriptions of Tyre's glory-it is like a "market place of nations" (23:3), a "joyous city" (23:7), a "crowning city," full of "princes" and the "honorable of the earth" (23:8). As a contrast, Isaiah describes the destruction of Tyre-a defiled woman (23:9), who is weak (23:10), shamed (without children; 23:4), and a ruin (23:13). The Lord is responsible for planning Tyre's devastation (23:9), using Assyria's armies to accomplish his plan (23:13). The destruction of Tyre is a type of the destruction of the wicked-and their wealth-at the time of the Lord's return.
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.205

quotes.text: The nail quite often in the sure place… was a nail in the tent on the pole, fastened to the sure place where you put things that you wanted to keep there
